title: Sandbox
Induction
Transport Lemmata
text:
%% meta 
%% types and terms 
%% Fin 
%% field accessors 
%% universes 
%% unit type 
%% empty type 
%% Bool 
%% sums 
%% identity type 
%% functions 
%% equivalences 
%% truncations 
%% quotients 
%% propositions 
%% loops 
%% aut 
%% dependent function 
%% dependent product 
%% propositions 


Transport lifts equalities in \(A\) to a function (in fact, an equivalence) between the fibres of a type family over \(A\). That is, for any type \(A\), path \(p:x=_Ay\), and dependent type family \(P:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\), transport along \(p\) is the term \[
    \operatorname{\mathsf{transport}}({P,p}):\equiv J(\lambda x\lambda p.P(a)\rightarrow P(x),\operatorname{\mathsf{id}}_{P(a)})(p)
\] We also write \[
    {p}^{\ast}(u):P(y)
\] for the transport of a term \(u:P(x)\) along \(p\).
Transport satisfies the following computation rule: \[
    \operatorname{\mathsf{transport}}({\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{x},u})=u
\]


\(P:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\), \(p:a=_Ab\), \(u:P(a)\), \(v:P(b)\)
\[
    u=v[P\downarrow p]\qquad\stackrel{\triangle}{=}\qquad\operatorname{\mathsf{transport}}({P,p})(u) = v
\]


If \(P:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\) is constant \(\lambda a.T\), then
\[
    \bigl(u=v\;[\lambda a.T\downarrow p]\bigr)\simeq(u=_Tv)
\]


If \(P:\mathcal{U}\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\) is the identity \(\lambda X.X\), then:
\[
    \bigl(A=B\;[\lambda X.X\downarrow p]\bigr)\simeq(A=_\mathcal{U}B)
\]
If \(P:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\) maps into an identity \(\lambda x.f(x)=g(x)\), where \(f,g:(a:A)\rightarrow B(a)\), then:
\[
    \bigl(u=v\;[\lambda x.f(x)=g(x))\downarrow p]\bigr)\simeq(\operatorname{\mathsf{apd}}_{f}({p})\cdot u\cdot\operatorname{\mathsf{apd}}_{g}({p}))
\]


\(f:B\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\), \(g:A\rightarrow B\), and \(P\stackrel{\triangle}{=}f\circ g:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\): \[
    \bigl(u=v\;[f\circ g\downarrow p]\bigr)\simeq(u=v\;[f\downarrow \operatorname{\mathsf{ap}}_{g}({p})])
\]


\(A:\mathcal{U}\) \(B:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\) \(P:(a:A)\times B(a)\) \(p:a=_Ab\) \(u:P(a)\) \(v:P(b)\) \(f:(x:B(a))\rightarrowC(a,x)\), \(g:(y:B(b))\rightarrow C(b,y)\) \[
    \bigl(x=y\;[B\downarrow p]\bigr)\simeq\bigl(f(x)=g(y)\;[P\downarrow\operatorname{pair{=}}(p,q)]\bigr)
\]

title: Homotopy Levels
text:
%% meta 
%% types and terms 
%% Fin 
%% field accessors 
%% universes 
%% unit type 
%% empty type 
%% Bool 
%% sums 
%% identity type 
%% functions 
%% equivalences 
%% truncations 
%% quotients 
%% propositions 
%% loops 
%% aut 
%% dependent function 
%% dependent product 
%% propositions 


A type \(A\) is contractible if there exists a centre of contraction \(c:A\) such that for every term \(x:A\), \(c=_Ax\): \[\begin{equation*}
    \textsf{isContra}(A):=\sum_{c:A}\prod_{x:A}c=_Ax
\end{equation*}\]
We define the homotopy level of a type as follows: \[\begin{align*}
    \textsf{is-($-2$)-type}(A)&:=\textsf{isContra}(A)\\
    \textsf{is-($n+1$)-type}(A)&:=\prod_{x,y:A}\textsf{is-$n$-type}(x=_Ay)
\end{align*}\] That is, a type is a (\(-2\))-type if it is contractible, and is an \(n\)-type if all of its equality types are (\(n-1\))-types.
We alias some common homotopy levels as follows: \[\begin{equation*}
    \textsf{isProp}(A)&:=\textsf{is-($-1$)-type}(A)\\
    \textsf{isSet}(A)&:=\textsf{is-$0$-type}(A)
\end{equation*}\]


For any type \(A\), \(\textsf{is-\)n\(-type}(A)\) is a proposition for all \(n\). \[\begin{equation*}
    \mathsf{isProp}\bigl(\textsf{is-$n$-type}(A)\bigr)
\end{equation*}\]


Homotopy levels are cumulative. That is:
\[\begin{equation*}
    \textsf{is-$n$-type}(A)\rightarrow\textsf{is-($n+1$)-type}(A)
\end{equation*}\]





Given a type \(T\), we define the type \(\operatorname{Aut}(T)\) of self equivalences of \(T\) as: \[\begin{align*}
    \operatorname{Aut}(T)&:=T\simeq T\\
    &\hphantom{:}=\sum_{f:T\rightarrow T}\mathsf{isEquiv}(f)%\\
    % &\hphantom{:}=\sum_{f:T\rightarrow T}\prod_{b:T}\mathsf{isContra}\sum_{a:T}f(a)=_Tb\\
    % &\hphantom{:}=\sum_{f:T\rightarrow T}\prod_{b:T}\sum_{c:\sum_{a:T}f(a)=_Tb}\prod_{p:\sum_{a:T}f(a)=_Tb}c=_{\sum_{a:T}f(a)=_Tb}p
\end{align*}\]


Given a type \(T\), we define the type \(\operatorname{BAut}(T)\) as: \[\begin{equation*}
    \operatorname{BAut}(T):=\sum_{X:\mathcal{U}}\|X=T\|_{-1}
\end{equation*}\]


If \(T\) is an \(n\)-type, then \(\operatorname{Aut}(T)\) is also an \(n\)-type.


Proof. Let \(\langle f,p\rangle,\langle g,q\rangle:\operatorname{Aut}(T)\).
Suppose \(T\) is contractible. Then, \[\begin{align*}
    \bigl(\langle f,p\rangle=\langle g,q\rangle\bigr)\simeq(P:f=g)\times\bigl(P_\ast(p)=q\bigr)
\end{align*}\] By assumption, \(T\) is contractible, so \(T\rightarrow T\) is contractible, and in particular, the identity type \(f=g\) is contractible. Since \(P_\ast(p)\) and \(q\) are propositions, their equality type is contractible. Hence, \(\operatorname{Aut}(T)\) is contractible.
Otherwise, suppose \(T\) is an \(n\)-type for \(n\geq-1\). Since \(\mathsf{isEquiv}(f)\) is a proposition for all \(f:T\rightarrow T\), the homotopy level of the dependent pair \(\sum_{f:T\rightarrow T}\mathsf{isEquiv}(f)\) is the maximum of \(-1\) and the homotopy level of \(T\rightarrow T\), the latter of which is the homotopy level of the codomain \(T\). Since \(n\geq-1\), \(\max(-1,n)=n\), and \(\operatorname{Aut}(T)\) is an \(n\)-type.


If \(T\) is an \(n\)-type, then \(\operatorname{BAut}(T)\) is an (\(n+1\))-type.


Proof. Let \(\langle X,p\rangle,\langle Y,q\rangle:\operatorname{BAut}(T)\). Then, \[\begin{equation*}
    \bigl(\langle X,p\rangle=\langle Y,q\rangle\bigr)\simeq\sum_{P:X=Y}P_\ast(p)=q
\end{equation*}\] Since \(P_\ast(p)\) and \(q\) are propositions, their equality is contractible, so the homotopy level of this dependent pair is the level of \(X=Y\).
Since being an \(n\)-type is a proposition, we may assume \(X\simeq T\) and \(Y\simeq T\). Since equivalences preserve homotopy level, \(X\) and \(Y\) are \(n\)-types. Combining the equivalences also yields \(X\simeq Y\). Since \(X,Y\) are \(n\)-types, \(X\simeq Y\) is an \(n\)-type, and thus by univalence, \((X=Y)\simeq(X\simeq Y)\) is an \(n\)-type. Hence \(\operatorname{BAut}(T)\) is an (\(n+1\))-type.


Given a pointed type \((A,a)\), we define the loop space \(\Omega A\) as the equality type: \[\begin{equation*}
    \Omega A:=(a=a)
\end{equation*}\]


For any \(T\), \[\begin{equation*}
    \Omega\operatorname{BAut}(T)\simeq\operatorname{Aut}(T)
\end{equation*}\] where \(\operatorname{BAut}(T)\) is pointed at \(|\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{}|\).


Proof. By the definition of a loop space, \[\begin{equation*}
    \Omega\operatorname{BAut}(T):=\bigl((T,|\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{}|)=(T,|\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{}|)\bigr)=\sum_{P:T=T}P_\ast(|\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{}|)=|\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{}|
\end{equation*}\] Since this is an equality of propositions, this sum is equivalent to \(T=T\). By univalence, \((T=T)\simeq(T\simeq T)=:\operatorname{Aut}(T)\).

title: Yoneda
text:
Definition 1 A natural transformation between two type families \(F,G:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\) is a family of maps \[
    \alpha:(x:A)\rightarrow(F(x)\rightarrow G(x))
\]
We denote the type of natural transformations between \(F\) and \(G\) by \(\mathrm{Nat}(F,G)\).


Definition 2 The Yoneda embedding \(よ_A:A\rightarrow(A\rightarrow\mathcal{U})\) is defined by \[
    よ_A(a):\equiv\lambda x.(a=x).
\]


Theorem 1 For any type family \(F:A\rightarrow\mathcal{U}\), the map \[
\begin{align*}
    f:\mathsf{Nat}(よ_A(a),F)&\rightarrow F(a)\\
    \alpha&\mapsto\alpha(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})
\end{align*}
\]
is an equivalence.


Proof. We construct the inverse map \[
\begin{align*}
    g:F(a)&\rightarrow\mathsf{Nat}(よ_A(a),F)\\
    u&\mapsto J\bigl(\lambda(x:A)\lambda(p:x=_Aa).F(x),u\bigr)
\end{align*}
\]
We show \(g\circ f=\operatorname{\mathsf{id}}_{\mathsf{Nat}(よ_A(a),F)}\). By \(\operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}\), it is sufficient to show: \[
    \bigl(\alpha:\mathsf{Nat}(よ_A(a),F)\bigr)\rightarrow (g\circ f)(\alpha)=\alpha
\]
but \(\alpha:(x:A)\rightarrow よ_A(a)(x)\rightarrow F(x)\) is a (higher) function, so by two more applications of \(\operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}\), we need: \[
    \bigl(\alpha:\mathsf{Nat}(よ_A(a),F)\bigr)\rightarrow(x:A)\rightarrow(p:a=_Ax)\rightarrow\bigl(g\circ f(\alpha)\bigr)(x,p)=\alpha(x,p)
\]
By based path induction on \(p\), it suffices to construct the method \(c_0:\bigl(g\circ f(\alpha)\bigr)(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})=\alpha(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})\); but this equality is judgemental: \[
\begin{align*}
    g\bigl(f(\alpha)\bigr)(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})&\equiv J(\lambda x\lambda p.f(\alpha))(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})\\
    &\equiv\alpha(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})\tag{$J_\beta$}
\end{align*}
\]
So the overall proof term is given by: \[
    \operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}\lambda\alpha.\operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}\operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}J\Bigl(\lambda x\lambda p.(g\circ f(\alpha)\bigr)(x)(p)=\alpha(x)(p),\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{\alpha(a)(\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})}\Bigr)
\]
To show \(f\circ g=\operatorname{\mathsf{id}}_{F(a)}\), we again reduce by \(\operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}\) to: \[
    (x:A)\rightarrow f\circ g(x)=x
\] \[
\begin{align*}
    (f\circ g)(u)&\equiv f\Bigl(J\bigl(\lambda x\lambda p.F(x),u\bigr)\Bigr)\\
    &\equiv\Bigl(J\bigl(\lambda x\lambda p.F(x),u\bigr)\Bigr)(a,\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{a})\\
    &\equiv u\tag{$J_\beta$}
\end{align*}
\] so the proof term is: \[
    \operatorname{\mathsf{funext}}(\operatorname{\mathsf{refl}}_{u})
\]


Introduction




This is another prooftree but in the margin. \[
\begin{prooftree}
  \AxiomC{ $P$ }
  \AxiomC{ $P \to Q$ }
  \BinaryInfC { $Q$ }
\end{prooftree}
\]
